title: Home
text: A Church in Revival A Pilot Proposed Draft status: this is the working draft (DEV). Every page here is under construction and subject to change. Nothing in it is a program yet — it is a proposal, offered for prayer and honest discussion. Two questions This little book asks a church — its pastors, its elders, its small group leaders, and any member willing to read along — two questions, and it asks them honestly, not rhetorically. The first question is: do you want a revival? Not "do you like the word." Most of us do. The question is whether we want the thing itself — God moving among us in a way we do not control, at a pace we do not set, with results we cannot take credit for. Revivals in the historical record are glorious, and they are also disruptive, humbling, and costly. Churches that received them were rearranged by them. So the first movement of this book slows down on the question itself, looks hard at what revival has actually been, and gives you room to answer not yet — and to see clearly what not yet costs. The second question is: if yes, what might it take to prepare the soil? Jesus told a story about a sower whose seed fell on four kinds of ground — the hard path, the rocky ground, the thorny ground, and the good soil. The seed was the same in all four. The difference was the soil. This book takes that story seriously as a description of a congregation, and it asks: what is our soil, honestly? Are there hungry hearts here — people quietly longing for more of God than Sunday is giving them? And what would it take to till the ground — before the rain comes, not after? Where this book comes from Behind this proposal stands a longer work, Intentional Journey of the Heart , which spent years cataloging what Scripture says about spiritual cause and effect — including a hard look at how revivals rise, and why they drain away. Two convictions from that work drive everything here: A desire for more. The ordinary believer can hear God's voice in Scripture and prayer and obey it. That is not a claim for special Christians; it is the normal Christian life, and many people in ordinary pews are hungrier for it than anyone has asked them about. A recognition of the drift downward. Every revival in the record eventually cooled. The fire came from God; the cooling followed patterns — recognizable, recurring, and to a sobering degree, preventable. A church that wants revival without studying the drift is asking for a gift it is not yet prepared to keep. This book is the meeting of those two convictions: the hunger, and the honesty. The shape of the book Movement I — Do You Want a Revival? The question, taken seriously. What revival is, what it costs, and what the record shows. Movement II — What Is Your Soil? An honest diagnosis — including a simple way to discover whether your congregation holds hungry hearts, and the guardrails that keep diagnosis from becoming surveillance. Movement III — Preparing the Soil. What a church actually does, starting with its own leadership — and how to build the vessel before the rain. Movement IV — Keeping What Comes. The failure modes of previous revivals, and what keeps the wineskin supple across years and generations. Reference. The deeper material behind each movement, and pointers into Intentional Journey of the Heart for those who want the full foundations. A word before you begin No book produces a revival. God does that, and He has never once been managed into it. What a church can do — what churches in the record demonstrably did — is prepare: soften the ground, gather the hungry, form the leaders, and learn the failure modes in advance. That preparation is commanded whether or not the rain comes on our schedule: "Sow for yourselves righteousness; reap steadfast love; break up your fallow ground, for it is the time to seek the LORD, that he may come and rain righteousness upon you" (Hosea 10:12, ESV). If that verse stirs something in you, start with Start Here, and then let Movement I ask

title: The Question
text: The Question Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Do you want a revival? Before you answer — and most of us answer this question in about half a second — let this chapter make one request: answer it slowly. The question deserves a slow answer, because it is not the question it appears to be. It appears to be a question about God, and whether we would like more of Him, and of course we would; who would say otherwise from a church pew? But asked honestly, it turns out to be a question about us — about our comfort, our calendars, our control, and what we are actually willing to have rearranged. That question takes longer than half a second, and it should. This book asked on its first page that both of its questions be taken "honestly, not rhetorically." Here is what that means for this one. A rhetorical question already contains its answer, and the asker is only waiting for you to say it. This question does not. There are real churches, led by faithful people, for whom the honest answer right now is not yet — and this chapter will treat that answer with respect, because Christ treats honest hearts with respect, and because a "yes" extracted by pressure is not a yes at all. Nothing here is designed to work you toward a foregone conclusion. Something here is designed to make sure that whatever you conclude, you concluded it with your eyes open. So: slowly, then. What is actually being asked? What we mean by revival By revival this book means something quite specific, and quite simple: God moving among His people in ways we do not control. Every word of that is doing work. God moving — the thing itself is His act, not ours; no committee has ever voted one into existence, and no committee ever will. Among His people — not first a wave of outsiders converted, though that often follows, but first a church awakened: cold hearts warmed, quiet prayers rekindled, sin long tolerated suddenly intolerable, the Scriptures alive in the hand again. And in ways we do not control — at a pace we do not set, through people we did not schedule, with results we cannot take credit for and could not have produced. Jesus said it of the Spirit's whole manner of working: "The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit" (John 3:8, ESV throughout). Revival is that wind arriving over a congregation at once. You can hear it. You cannot steer it. Scripture prays for exactly this, which is worth noticing: the longing is not a modern invention. "Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?" (Psalm 85:6). Notice who is being revived — your people , believers, the already gathered — and notice what the revival is for : not excitement, but joy in God Himself. Habakkuk prays the same way from the middle of a discouraging century: "O LORD, I have heard the report of you, and your work, O LORD, do I fear. In the midst of the years revive it; in the midst of the years make it known; in wrath remember mercy" (Habakkuk 3:2). He has heard the report — he knows what God has done before — and he asks for it again, in his own ordinary, unspectacular years. That is the prayer this book is circling: Lord, do among us what the report says You have done. And He has done it. The record is real. There have been seasons — in New England in the 1730s, in Wales in 1904, in the Hebrides, at Herrnhut, in a hundred smaller places that never made the histories — when God compressed into months what usually takes generations. Jonathan Edwards, a careful and rather unexcitable observer, counted more than three hundred souls brought in within about half a year, in a town of about eleven hundred people. Whole communities changed temperature. The next chapter of this movement walks through that record honestly — what actually happened, what lasted, what didn't, and why — so we will not spend it here. For now the point

title: The Seven Letters: What Christ Says to a Church
text: The Seven Letters: What Christ Says to a Church Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. This book opened with a question: do you want a revival? It would be fair to wonder whether that question is a device — a writer's way of getting your attention before the real material starts. It is not a device, and here is the reason. The question has already been asked. Not by an author, not by a conference speaker, not by a denominational survey — by the risen Christ, in writing, to seven real congregations, and the letters are still in your Bible. Revelation chapters 2 and 3 contain seven short letters, dictated by the risen Lord to the apostle John, each addressed to a particular church in a particular city in Roman Asia — Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea. Real places. Real congregations, with real reputations, real pressures, and real problems, some of which they knew about and some of which they did not. These letters are the closest thing we have to hearing Christ conduct a church review. Before this book asks anything of you, it wants you to sit with what He asked of them. He writes to churches, not only to Christians The first thing to notice about the letters is easy to miss because we are so used to reading the Bible as individuals: each letter is addressed to a congregation , and it speaks to that congregation as one. "I have this against you, that you have abandoned the love you had at first" (Revelation 2:4). That sentence is not addressed to a person. It is addressed to the church at Ephesus — the whole body, as a body. In the original language the "you" is singular: Christ speaks to the congregation the way you would speak to one soul. A church, in these letters, has works. It has a reputation. It has a temperature. It has a history it is accountable for — "remember therefore from where you have fallen" — and a future that depends on what it does next. Christ treats each congregation as something that can be praised, warned, and held responsible as itself , not merely as a container full of separate believers. This should not entirely surprise us, because it is how God has always spoken. The prophets addressed whole cities and nations as moral agents — Amos read out indictments against nation after nation; Nineveh repented as a city under Jonah's preaching. Jesus did the same in His earthly ministry: He pronounced woes on Chorazin and Bethsaida as towns, and He wept over Jerusalem as one being with one will — "and you were not willing " (Matthew 23:37). The seven letters are not an oddity. They are the risen Lord continuing a practice that runs through the whole of Scripture: God deals with His people not only one by one but together — and He holds the together accountable. And yet — hold this alongside it, because the letters hold both — every one of the seven letters ends by turning to the individual. "He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches" (Revelation 2:7). Each letter closes with a promise not to the church but to "the one who conquers." The diagnosis is corporate; the response is personal. No one in Sardis could hide behind the congregation's deadness, and no one in Sardis was doomed by it either — "you have still a few names in Sardis, people who have not soiled their garments" (Revelation 3:4). A faithful person can walk in white inside a dying church. Christ never lets the church's condition override a single willing heart. That double address matters for everything this book will ask later. When we talk about the spiritual condition of a congregation, we are not inventing a category. We are standing on ground the Lord Himself staked out: churches have conditions, Christ reads them accurately, and He says so — while leaving every individual member free to hear and respond. How He examines: praise, diagnosis, prescription, promise The second thing to notice is how Christ goes about it. The seven letters are not s

title: What the Record Shows
text: What the Record Shows Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. The opening of this movement asked you a question — do you want a revival? — and asked you not to answer it too quickly. This chapter exists to slow the answer down further still, in the most useful way possible: by looking at what actually happened. Because the question is dangerously easy to answer from imagination. Imagination supplies two pictures, and both are wrong. One is the romantic picture — the packed church, the weeping crowd, the town transformed forever, roll credits. The other is the cynical picture — emotionalism, manipulation, a tent and a bus ticket, nothing left by Tuesday. The record supports neither. What the record actually shows is stranger, more glorious, and more sobering than either: the fire is real, and the fire drains. Genuine moves of God have swept through real towns and changed real populations — and every one of them eventually cooled, and the cooling followed patterns you can learn. So this chapter walks the record: what genuine revivals looked like when they arose, what they did to the churches and towns that received them, the recurring shape of their rise, and the recurring shape of their cooling. Where the evidence is strong, we will say so plainly. Where it is thin or contested, we will hedge out loud. You should finish this chapter both sobered and hungry — because both responses are honest, and both of them argue for the same thing: preparation. When the Fire Fell Start where the church starts. At Pentecost, three thousand people were cut to the heart in a single day — not a good Sunday, a change in the spiritual state of a crowd, all at once, in public. Whatever else revival is, Scripture's founding instance says it is possible : God can move on a multitude in an afternoon. And the record after Scripture says He has kept doing it. In Jonathan Edwards's Northampton, the pastor himself did the counting: "more than 300 souls" brought in, by his reckoning, in the space of half a year — in a town of about eleven hundred people. Sit with that arithmetic for a moment. That is not a successful sermon series. That is a whole community changing state, the way water changes state, visibly, over months, with the town's own minister watching it happen house by house. At Cane Ridge, Kentucky, in 1801, the crowd that gathered was estimated near a tenth of Kentucky's entire population. One meeting; a tenth of a state. In Wales in 1904, the awakening moved so fast and so publicly that the daily newspapers carried it — revival as front page news, spreading down the valleys ahead of any preacher. In the Hebrides, before the preacher ever arrived, two elderly sisters and a barn full of praying men had been interceding — the fire fell on ground that had been soaked in prayer first. At Herrnhut, a covenanted community — a people who had bound themselves together before God — experienced what their own record calls a Pentecost of their own, following a season of covenant. At Azusa Street, weeks of prayer preceded the outpouring, and then a newspaper carried word of it around the world. And within living memory: at Asbury in 2023, a routine chapel service simply did not end, and over sixteen days tens of thousands came — word traveling this time not by newspaper but by TikTok. William James, who studied this phenomenon carefully a century ago, described the sudden work of God in a surrendered soul as breaking through all barriers and sweeping in "like a sudden flood." Every generation of witnesses says some version of the same thing. Whatever explanation anyone prefers, the events are not in doubt: there are moments in history when God moves on a whole community at once, and the community knows it. That is the first thing the record shows, and a church asking "do we want this?" should hear it clearly: this really happens. Not only in Acts. In Massachusetts, in Kentucky, in Wales, in the Hebrides, in Saxony, in

title: Movement I — Do You Want a Revival?
text: Movement I — Do You Want a Revival? Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. The question sounds devotional. It is actually one of the most searching questions a church can be asked, and this movement exists to keep it from being answered too quickly. What this movement holds: The question itself — an opening chapter that asks it pastorally and honestly: what we mean by revival, what we do not mean, and why "yes" must be allowed to cost something before it counts. What the record shows — the revival model tested against history: how genuine moves of God arose, what they did to the churches that received them, and the drift by which every one of them eventually cooled. (Standalone treatment here; full foundations in Intentional Journey of the Heart , Volume 3.) The letters to the seven churches — the risen Christ's own diagnostics of real congregations (Revelation 2–3): churches praised, churches warned, and one church that had everything but its first love. The question "do you want a revival?" turns out to be a question Christ has already asked churches directly — and He asked it of whole congregations, not just individuals. The honest "not yet" — what it means, and what it costs, to see the state of things clearly and decide to wait. This movement ends with the reader free: no manipulation, no manufactured urgency. A revival wanted for the wrong reasons is one of the failure modes Movement IV catalogs. The movement closes by handing the reader who says yes — or even I want to want it — to Movement II: before you can prepare soil, you have to know what soil you have.

title: The Four Soils
text: The Four Soils Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Movement I ended with the risen Christ examining seven congregations and telling each one the truth about itself. If you have come this far, you have accepted the premise that a church has a condition — something Christ can read, praise, warn, and hold responsible as itself. Movement II asks the next question: can we read it? Not with His eyes — we will come to that limit before this chapter ends — but honestly and safely: can a church look at itself and see something true? Jesus gave us the picture to look with. He told one story that He flagged as the key to all the others — "Do you not understand this parable? How then will you understand all the parables?" (Mark 4:13) — and it is a story about ground. A sower went out to sow. Some seed fell along the hard path, and the birds ate it. Some fell on rocky ground, sprang up fast, and withered in the sun for lack of root. Some fell among thorns, and the thorns choked it. And some fell on good soil and bore fruit — a hundredfold, sixty, thirty (Matthew 13:3–8). Then the words that aim the story at you: "He who has ears, let him hear" (Matthew 13:9). The same sower. The same seed. Four outcomes — and the entire difference was the soil. We usually read this parable one heart at a time, and we should; that is how Jesus explained it. But this book has been arguing since its first page that the parable also describes a congregation — that a church, taken whole, has a soil condition. This chapter walks the four soils at that scale. It is offered as a mirror. Please hear that word now, because everything here depends on it, and we will say it again before we are done: a mirror is something you stand in front of. It is not something you hold up to other people. The story Jesus told, and what He said it meant Stay with the individual reading for a moment, because Jesus' own interpretation is the foundation for everything else. He explained each soil, and each explanation names not just that the word failed but where and why . The path. "When anyone hears the word of the kingdom and does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what has been sown in his heart" (Matthew 13:19). The seed never gets in. There is no crack in the surface. The word is heard, and nothing enters. Hearing happened; receiving did not. The rocky ground. "This is the one who hears the word and immediately receives it with joy, yet he has no root in himself, but endures for a while, and when tribulation or persecution arises on account of the word, immediately he falls away" (Matthew 13:20–21). Notice how generous the description is: the reception is immediate and joyful — nothing about it fake. But the joy is fed from outside — novelty, belonging — and when the outside turns hostile, there is nothing inside to draw on. "No root in himself" is the diagnosis. The plant was real. It was just shallow. The thorns. "This is the one who hears the word, but the cares of the world and the deceitfulness of riches choke the word, and it proves unfruitful" (Matthew 13:22). The thorns get their own section shortly, because the parable gives them a rank the church has rarely given them. For now, notice only this: of the three failures, the thorny ground is the only one where the word lives . It takes root. It grows. It is genuine. And it is slowly strangled by things that are not, in themselves, sins — cares and riches, worry and comfort. Luke widens the list: "they are choked by the cares and riches and pleasures of life, and their fruit does not mature" (Luke 8:14). Not killed. Choked. The plant is still standing there, green and fruitless. The good soil. "This is the one who hears the word and understands it. He indeed bears fruit" (Matthew 13:23). Luke adds three qualities worth engraving somewhere: the good soil is those who, "hearing the word, hold it fast in an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with patience" (Luke

title: Hungry Hearts
text: Hungry Hearts Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Everything in this movement has been building to one question. We have looked at the four soils. We have admitted that a congregation is not simply the average of its members, and that the counters — attendance, giving, participation — cannot see what we most need to see. All of that was preparation for this: Does our soil include hearts that are hungry for more of God? Not "do we have committed members." Not "is our attendance healthy." Not "are people serving." Those questions have their place, and every church already knows how to ask them. This question is different, and most churches have never asked it out loud: somewhere in our pews — maybe third row on the left, maybe running the sound board, maybe teaching the four year olds — are there people who are quietly, privately hungry for more of God than they are currently getting? Here is why the question matters so much. Every revival in the record began with hungry people. Not with programs, not with campaigns — with a handful of hearts that ached for God and started seeking Him like people who mean it. The hunger came first; the rain found the hunger. And Jesus made hunger itself a promise: "Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied" (Matthew 5:6, ESV). Notice what He blesses. Not the full. Not the satisfied. The hungry. If your congregation contains hungry hearts, you are already holding the most precious thing a preparing church can hold — and you probably do not know who they are. This chapter is about how to find out. And — this is the part that makes it different from every survey you have ever run — how the very act of finding out becomes the first step of feeding them. What hunger sounds like in an ordinary pew Start by learning to recognize the thing itself, because spiritual hunger almost never announces itself in spiritual language. Nobody stands up in the announcements and says, "I am starving for God." Hunger in an ordinary pew sounds like this: It sounds like longing. A woman who has been in the church thirty years says, almost apologetically, "I read my Bible, I pray, I come every week — but I remember when it felt alive , and I miss that." She is not complaining about the church. She is homesick for God. It sounds like restlessness. A man in his forties who serves on two committees says, "I don't know. I keep thinking I should be doing something more — not busier. More. Does that make sense?" It makes perfect sense. Restlessness of that particular kind is often desire that has not yet found its name. It sounds like the sentence "there must be more than Sunday." Sometimes it is said with a laugh, sometimes with an edge, sometimes with tears that surprise the person crying them. A teenager says it about youth group. A grandmother says it about the whole shape of her spiritual life. When you hear that sentence — there has to be more than this — do not rush to defend Sunday. You may be listening to John 10:10 waking up in a person: Jesus said He came that they may have life, and have it abundantly, and something in this person has just noticed the gap between that promise and their experience. The gap hurts. The hurt is hunger. And it sounds like dryness that aches. This one you must get right, because instinct will get it wrong. Some of your most spiritually hungry people currently feel nothing — prayer flat, Scripture flat, worship flat — and they have quietly concluded that the dryness means something is broken in them. Here is the thing your leaders must know: dryness that aches — where the person misses God, mourns the distance, keeps showing up in anguish — is very often not a sign of a heart going cold. It is frequently the sign of a heart very much alive, in a season God Himself is using. The drifting soul does not ache. The hungry one does. We give this a whole chapter later in the book, When the Ground Goes Dry, bec

title: The Guardrails
text: The Guardrails Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Before this movement offers you a single diagnostic — before the Four Connects, before any conversation about hungry hearts, before anyone in your church asks anyone else how they connect with God — this chapter has to happen. It is the conscience of the book. Everything Movement II proposes, and everything Movement III builds on it, is bound by the commitments on this page, and if a church cannot keep these commitments, we would rather it never used the diagnostics at all. Here is why. This book asks a church to look at its own soil, and soil, in Jesus' parable, means hearts. The moment a church starts learning about its people's hearts, it is holding something powerful — more powerful than a budget, more intimate than a membership roll. Church history and recent memory both testify that knowledge of hearts, in institutional hands, does not stay neutral. It drifts. It becomes a way of sorting people, then a way of managing them, then a way of controlling them — and the drift happens slowly enough that everyone involved still believes they are shepherding. The review process behind this book demanded these guardrails, and demanded that they be written down, because unwritten limits do not hold. Good intentions are not a guardrail. A sentence you can be held to is. So here are the commitments. They are written in the first person plural on purpose: this is what we — this book, this pilot, and any church that takes it up — bind ourselves to. Read them before you read anything else in this movement, and come back to them whenever anything later in the book seems to loosen them. Nothing later in the book loosens them. 1. Mirrors, not levers In this pilot, anything a church learns about its people's hearts is held for them, never over them. There are two things you can do with knowledge about a person's soul. You can hold it up as a mirror — here is what we saw, offered back to you, so that you can see yourself and grow — or you can pick it up as a lever — a way to move people, sort them, promote them, sideline them, manage them. The mirror serves the person. The lever serves the institution. And the tragedy is that the same piece of knowledge can be either one; the difference is entirely in the hands that hold it. So we draw the line at the start, in writing: soil knowledge is a mirror for the community, never a lever for the management. If your congregation learns, through the conversations this book proposes, that many of its people feel far from God — that knowledge exists to help those people , and for no other purpose. It is not market research. It is not a performance dashboard for the staff. It is not raw material for a strategic plan that treats hungry hearts as an asset class. The moment anyone in leadership catches themselves thinking of what they have learned about someone's walk with God as a management resource , this commitment has been breached, and the right response is to stop and repent, not to press on with better intentions. Peter's charge to elders is the standing text over this whole chapter: shepherd the flock of God that is among you, "not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock" (1 Peter 5:3, ESV). Paul says the same thing about his own apostolic authority: "Not that we lord it over your faith, but we work with you for your joy" (2 Corinthians 1:24, ESV). If an apostle would not use what he knew of a church's faith as a lever, neither will we. 2. No scores In this pilot, nothing about anyone's interior life is ever graded, ranked, or tied to money, position, or standing. No one gets a spiritual maturity score. No one is ranked against anyone else. No leadership appointment, no ministry role, no salary, no budget line, no public honor, and no quiet loss of standing is ever attached to what anyone discovers about the condition of anyone's heart. This is absolute, and it is absolute for

title: Movement II — What Is Your Soil?
text: Movement II — What Is Your Soil? Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Jesus' parable of the sower describes four soils — the hard path, the rocky ground, the thorny ground, and the good soil — and the difference between them decided everything the seed could do. This movement applies that description to a congregation, honestly and safely. What this movement holds: The four soils as a congregation's self portrait — hardness, shallowness, thorns (and the parable puts riches and comfort first among the thorns), and good ground; what each looks like at the scale of a whole church, and why a congregation is not simply the average of its members. The hungry hearts question. The single most important diagnostic in this book: do we have soil that includes hearts hungry for more of God? The instrument offered here is the Four Connects — a simple, teachable way of asking how a person connects with God (and noticing when they long for more than they currently have). Used well, the Four Connects do double duty: they find the hungry hearts a congregation already contains, and they offer those same hearts a path forward. Diagnosis and invitation in one motion. The limits of counting. Why attendance, giving, and program participation cannot tell you what you need to know — the person who performs the behaviors and the person whose heart is transformed look identical to every counter. What actually reveals soil: patient observation, honest conversation, fruit over time. The guardrails. This page will be non negotiable and will sit beside every diagnostic in the book: what a church learns about its people's hearts is held for them, never over them. Mirrors for the community, never levers for management. No scores attached to money, position, or standing; no one's interior life reported upward without their knowledge and consent; and one test every leadership must run on itself — what happened to the last person who told us something we did not want to hear? The movement closes at the threshold of action: you know your soil. Movement III is about tilling it — and it starts in a place most churches do not expect.

title: It Starts at the Top
text: It Starts at the Top Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Everything in this movement — the tilling practices, the vessel building, the pilot itself — waits behind a single door, and the door is this chapter. If Movement II found hungry hearts in your congregation, the natural instinct is to go serve them: announce something, launch something, put the hungry into groups and get started. This chapter asks you not to. Not yet. Because there is a first plot of ground that has to be broken before any other plot will take seed, and it is not in the pews. It is the leadership's own soil. Here is the conviction this chapter carries, stated as plainly as we can state it: this has to start with the leadership of the church, who should be a fellowship of the heart already — and whatever it takes for that culture change to happen at the top is the actual beginning of revival preparation. Not a preliminary to the beginning. The beginning. A church can skip this step; churches do it all the time. What it cannot do is skip this step and still arrive where it meant to go, because a congregation will not go where its leadership has not gone. It never has. There is no recorded exception, and there is a reason there is no recorded exception, which is where we have to start. Sheep read shepherds, not memos Every church runs two curricula at the same time. The first is the stated one: the sermons, the mission statement, the discipleship pathway on the website, the values painted on the lobby wall. The second is the lived one: what the leaders actually do, what they actually love, what they make time for, what they avoid, what happens to their faces when someone tells them the truth. And in every congregation on earth, when the two curricula disagree, the people learn the second one. Not because they are cynical. Because they are sheep, and sheep read shepherds. It is how God built them. Jesus said it in one sentence: "A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher" (Luke 6:40, ESV). Notice what the verse does not say. It does not say the disciple will be like the teacher's teaching . It says the disciple will be like the teacher — the person, the actual life, the real interior condition leaking out through a thousand small unguarded moments. Full training does not produce people shaped like your doctrinal statement. It produces people shaped like you. Peter, writing to elders, gives the same truth from the shepherd's side: shepherd the flock of God that is among you, "not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock" (1 Peter 5:3, ESV). The instrument of leadership Peter names is not authority, though elders have it, and not eloquence, though some have that too. It is example . The elder leads by being, visibly, a certain kind of person — which means the elder's own walk with God is not his private business with a public role attached. His walk with God is the curriculum. So is yours. This is why programs alone have never prepared a church for revival and never will. A program is a memo. You can staff it, fund it, announce it beautifully — and the congregation will look straight past it at the leaders and ask the only question sheep ever really ask: is this real in them? If the answer is yes, the program almost doesn't matter; the life will spread by contact. If the answer is no, the program almost doesn't matter either. Not run one — be one So what, concretely, is being asked of a leadership team here? Not that the pastors and elders administer a fellowship of the heart initiative. Not that they approve it, resource it, or even champion it from the front. All of that is running a fellowship. The ask is different in kind: that the leadership circle become one. That the pastors and elders of the church meet together, regularly and for a long season, as a small group under exactly the same disciplines they will later invite the co

title: The Vessel Before the Rain
text: The Vessel Before the Rain Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. There is a moment in the Gospels when Jesus, pressed about why His disciples were feasting while others fasted, reached for an image from every hearer's pantry: "no one puts new wine into old wineskins. If he does, the new wine will burst the skins and it will be spilled... But new wine must be put into fresh wineskins" (Luke 5:37–38, ESV). He was saying something about the newness of what He brought. But the image carries a second lesson that the history of revival has underlined in red, over and over, for three hundred years: when God pours, the question of the vessel has already been decided. The wine does not wait while you sew. Whatever skins exist at the moment of pouring — those are the skins that will hold it, or fail to. This chapter is about sewing the skins now. The previous chapter argued that preparation starts with the leadership's own soil — a leadership that has itself become a fellowship of the heart, or nothing else in this movement will hold. This chapter is the second build, and it is the most practical proposal in this book: form a bench of ordinary members into small group companions and leaders ahead of need — before there is any visible need at all — so that if God stirs a hundred hearts in your congregation, there is somewhere for every one of them to land on day two. Nowhere to Put the Harvest Movement IV of this book opens the case file on how revivals were actually lost, and if you have not read How Revivals Were Lost yet, it is worth reading alongside this chapter — because this chapter exists to answer that one. Of the failure modes in the record, the one the numbers document best is the one that chapter calls awakened, and never formed : real fire, real stirring, thousands of genuine beginnings — and then the beginnings evaporating, not because the awakening was false, but because the churches that received it had nowhere to put it. The pattern is painfully consistent. Vast crowds stirred at Cane Ridge in 1801, and a generation of ministers spent the following decades lamenting how few of the awakened became established Christians. Tens of thousands recorded decisions in the great twentieth century crusades, and the churches of the host cities gained a small fraction of them. And the finding inside the finding — the one every church praying for revival should write on the wall of its meeting room — is that retention tracked the follow up structure , not the size or heat of the meeting. Where a newly awakened person was met within days by a companion, a small circle, and a place to belong, the great majority stayed and grew. Where they were handed a booklet and a service time, the decision faded like a seed on rock — which is exactly the diagnosis Jesus gave in advance: seed that springs up immediately, with joy, and withers, "since it had no root" (Matthew 13:5–6, ESV). Now do the arithmetic that the record forces on us. An awakening does not produce mature believers. It produces beginnings — hundreds of them, sometimes in weeks. Every one of those beginnings needs what a stirred crowd does not automatically contain: a person alongside, a small community, a path, a practice. And here is the hard part — the people able to provide that cannot be produced during the awakening. Forming a believer into someone who can sit at the front of a small circle, hold it steady, and walk a newcomer through hearing God in Scripture takes months of formation and, before that, months of being formed themselves. You cannot compress it into the two weeks after the fire falls, any more than a farmer can build barns during the harvest. When the rain comes, the vessel question is already answered. The only variable is when you answered it. That is the whole argument of this chapter, and it fits in a sentence: because the harvest cannot wait for the vessel, the vessel must be built before the harvest. The Bench So wh

title: The Practices That Till
text: The Practices That Till Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Somewhere in the last two chapters, a question has probably been forming in you, and it is the right question: all right — but what do we actually do? The leadership has begun its own tilling. The hungry hearts are being found, one unhurried conversation at a time, and each of them is being offered a path built on the Four Connects — with self, with others, with God, with mission (Hungry Hearts laid out that whole map). Now comes the practical center of this movement: the practices themselves. The ordinary, repeatable things a person — and a fellowship, and eventually a congregation — actually does, week by week, so that hard ground softens. Before naming them, one thing must be settled, because everything else in this chapter hangs on it. One seed, many tillings There is one seed in this book, and only one: hearing and obeying God. Not hearing about Him — hearing Him, above all in Scripture, and doing what He says. That is the life the ordinary believer is invited into; it is what every hungry heart in your pews is actually hungry for, whether or not they have the words; and it is what every revival in the record was, at its core — a great many people beginning to hear and obey God at once. Everything in this chapter serves that one seed. The practices below are not five separate spiritual projects, five boxes to check, five more things for busy people to feel guilty about. They are five ways of tending the same ground so that one seed can live in it. A farmer does many different things in a field — plowing, watering, weeding, waiting — but he is not raising five crops. He is raising one, and every motion of his hands serves it. Hold the practices that way. When any of them becomes an end in itself — when a person starts being good at devotions rather than coming to hear God — the practice has quietly stopped tilling and started decorating. What the practices cannot do — and why that is good news Now the boundary, and it must be stated plainly, because more spiritual damage has been done by forgetting it than by almost anything else a sincere church can do. The practices do not produce life. They tend conditions. God gives the growth. The longer work behind this book, which studied these practices in depth, states the boundary in one sentence: formation tends conditions; the Spirit gives the growth. That is not a disclaimer tacked on for humility's sake. It is the load bearing wall of the whole chapter, and Scripture sets it exactly: "So neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything, but only God who gives the growth" (1 Corinthians 3:7, ESV). Paul had planted; Apollos had watered; the labor was real and necessary — and the decisive act belonged to neither of them. Jesus made the same point in a parable that ought to hang over every discipleship program ever devised: a man scatters seed, sleeps and rises night and day, "and the seed sprouts and grows; he knows not how" (Mark 4:27, ESV). The farmer's work fills the parable. The growth is not his. Why is this good news rather than discouragement? Two reasons. First, it takes the crushing weight off. If the practices produced life, then a season when they feel lifeless would mean you are failing, and a person who prays would be manufacturing God's presence like a product. Neither is true. Your part is to show up in the field — to read, to listen, to obey, to gather, to serve. The germination was never your job. You can stop straining at what was never asked of you. Second, it protects the church from its oldest counterfeit. A congregation that forgets this boundary will eventually start measuring devotional output and calling it maturity — mistaking the watering can for the harvest. The person who does all the practices and the person whose heart is being transformed can look identical from the outside; only the boundary keeps a church honest about the difference. We till.

title: When the Ground Goes Dry
text: When the Ground Goes Dry Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion, not yet a program. Here is a promise this book can make with complete confidence: if your church begins seriously praying and preparing for revival, some of you will go dry. Not might. Will. The record is unanimous. People who press in toward God — who trade a comfortable Sunday faith for real seeking — pass through seasons when prayer feels like talking to the ceiling, when the Scriptures that once glowed lie flat on the page, when the God they were just beginning to hear seems to have stepped out of the room. And here is the danger: dryness is the most misread experience in the whole spiritual life. Misread it one way, and you crush a soul God is carrying. Misread it the other way, and you comfort a soul God is calling to wake up. So this chapter exists to prevent one error above all others — the one unforgivable pastoral mistake in a preparing church: telling a dry believer that their dryness means they are disconnected from God. Say that to the wrong person and you have handed the enemy his favorite weapon, sharpened. Because dryness is not one thing. It is at least four things, nearly identical from the outside — same flat prayers, same dull Bible, same absent feelings — while calling for responses so different that two of them are exact opposites. A church that intends to pray its way toward revival must teach its leaders to tell these four apart, because instinct will not do it. Instinct says feelings gone, God gone. And instinct, at the exact moment God is doing His deepest work in a person, is usually wrong. Four kinds of dry Walk a farm in a drought and the fields all look the same: brown, cracked, still. But a farmer knows the ground goes dry for different reasons — one field was never watered, one was worked to exhaustion, one has something poisoning the soil, and one has been deliberately left fallow so it can bear more later. Same appearance; four stories; four remedies. The soul is like that. The tradition of the church — tested across centuries and, remarkably, confirmed by modern research on spiritual struggle — distinguishes four kinds of dryness: 1. Neglect — the ground nobody watered. The believer has quietly stopped using the ordinary means of grace: Scripture, prayer, worship, honest fellowship. The dryness is simple thirst, and the remedy is simple drinking. 2. Over obligation — the ground worked to death. The believer has turned faith into a crushing ledger of duties, or serves in a driven, restless overwork that has burned the joy out of everything. The remedy is not more effort; it is rest and a lighter yoke. 3. The enemy's desolation — a dry wind sent against the soul. Discouragement with a voice in it, whispering lies, pushing the believer to quit and to reverse good decisions. The remedy is to resist — hold course and refuse the counsel. 4. God's own stripping — the fallow field; what the old writers called the dark night. God Himself withdraws the felt sweetness of His presence, not because He has left, but because He is weaning the soul off the sweetness so it can learn to love Him for Himself. The remedy is to receive — wait, stay faithful, and let Him work. Look hard at the last two, because this is where the stakes live. The enemy's desolation is to be fought. God's stripping is to be welcomed. Misdiagnose, and you invert the response — you end up fighting what God is doing, or surrendering to what the enemy is doing. A leader who has only one category — all dryness is backsliding, or all dryness is a holy dark night — will eventually do real harm with the best of intentions. And one more thing must be said before the four cases, and it belongs in bold: sometimes the dryness is not spiritual at all. Depression wears the same gray coat. We will come to that squarely, because getting it wrong there is not a pastoral error but a medical one. The one question that sorts them: which way does the d

title: The Pilot, Season by Season
text: The Pilot, Season by Season Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Everything in this book so far has been conviction and diagnosis. Movement I asked whether you want a revival at all. Movement II asked what your soil honestly is, and how to find the hungry hearts in it without harming them. This movement has walked the preparation itself — the leadership first, the vessel before the rain, the practices that till, the dry seasons rightly read. Now comes the part a proposal owes you: what a willing church would actually run . What follows is that proposal, laid out season by season. Read it with two rules in hand, because they govern everything on this page. Seasons, not dates. Nothing here has a calendar attached. A season is done when its work is done, and its work is done in hearts, which do not keep schedules. One church may walk Season One in four months; another may need a year and be the healthier for taking it. The pilot has an order — the order is most of the message — but it has no clock. The farmer in James waits for the precious fruit of the earth, "being patient about it, until it receives the early and the late rains" (James 5:7, ESV). Patience is not the pilot's weakness. It is one of its load bearing walls. Gates, not deadlines. Each season ends not with a date but with a question — asked out loud, answered honestly, in prayer. And at every gate, "not yet" is a fully honorable answer. Not a failure, not a verdict: an honest reading of the ground, and often the most obedient sentence in the room. A church that says "not yet" at a gate and keeps working the season it is in has lost nothing. A church that pushes through a gate it has not honestly passed has planted its later failure at the exact spot where it skipped the question. One more word before the seasons: adapt freely. This is a proposal, not a franchise. Your church's size, polity, history, and wounds are yours; the shape below is offered for testing, and it expects your hands on it. What should survive every adaptation is the order, the gates, and the guardrails. Everything else is yours to fit. Season Zero — The Decision Purpose. To answer this book's title question honestly, as a leadership, before anyone announces anything — because a revival prayed for casually is a tower begun without an estimate. What actually happens. The leadership — pastors, elders, and whoever genuinely carries the spiritual weight of the church, whatever your polity calls them — agrees to read the first two movements of this book together. One movement per meeting: an evening on Movement I, an evening on Movement II. Read beforehand, gather to talk honestly, and pray — actually pray, not open and close pray. No congregational announcement. No vision series. No committee. Just the people responsible for the flock, sitting with the question the book asks and refusing to answer it rhetorically. The conversation to have is the one Jesus prescribed for exactly this moment: "For which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost, whether he has enough to complete it? Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him, saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish'" (Luke 14:28–30, ESV). Count the cost, out loud, with the record open. Revival, if God sends it, will rearrange this church — its calendar, its comfort, its leadership's private lives first of all. Movement I laid out what the fire has actually cost the churches that received it. Movement II laid out what an honest look at your own soil will require, and the guardrails that looking demands. The question on the table is not "do we like this book." It is: do we want the thing itself? Expect this season to be quieter and harder than it sounds. Some leaderships will discover, to their joy, a hunger they had never said out loud to each other. Some will discover honest hesitation — a member of t

title: Movement III — Preparing the Soil
text: Movement III — Preparing the Soil Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. If Movement II found hungry hearts in your congregation, this movement is about what to do — and the order in which to do it. The order is the message. What this movement holds: It starts with the leadership — or it does not start. The first soil to be tilled is the leadership's own. Before a church asks its members to open their hearts, its pastors and elders become a fellowship of the heart themselves: a small circle that actually practices hearing God together, tells one another the truth, and lets the Word reach their calendars and budgets and not just their doctrine. Whatever culture change that requires is the beginning of revival preparation — there is no route around it, because a congregation will not go where its leadership has not gone. Build the vessel before the rain. Revivals in the record were lost partly because the churches that received them had nowhere to put what God sent — thousands awakened, and no formed leaders to walk with them. The answer proposed here is to develop small group leadership ahead of time : a bench of ordinary members formed into companions and group leaders before they are needed. The mechanics for this exist and are already piloted — Fellowship of the Heart is the working model this book leans on, and this movement shows how a church would run that preparation as the second stage of the pilot. The practices that till. Hearing and obeying God as the one seed everything else serves; the simple practices — Scripture, listening prayer, honest fellowship — by which ground softens; and the Four Connects as the offered path for every hungry heart Movement II surfaced. When the ground goes dry. A praying church will pass through dryness, and dryness is the most misread experience in the spiritual life. This chapter gives leaders the differential — the four different things dryness can be, two of which call for opposite responses — so that a church preparing for revival does not quit at the exact moment God is doing its deepest work. The movement closes with the pilot itself: what a willing church would actually run, season by season — prepare, test, and get started.

title: The Drift Downward
text: The Drift Downward Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. The first page of this book named its two convictions in a single breath: a desire for more, and a recognition of the drift downward. Every movement so far has been unfolding the desire. This chapter unfolds the drift — because a church that wants revival without understanding the drift is asking for a gift it is not yet prepared to keep. Start with three scenes you have probably witnessed. A believer — earnest, once on fire — notices one day that her prayer has gone quiet. Not stopped, exactly. Thinned. She cannot name the month it happened. A family — church every Sunday for three generations — discovers that the grandchildren keep the building and the vocabulary but not the thing itself. Nobody rebelled. It just faded. A congregation — busy, solvent, respected — has more meetings than it had twenty years ago and less fire. The old members can name the year the temperature changed. Nobody else noticed anything change at all. Three problems, we assume. Three committees, probably. A discipleship problem, a family ministry problem, a church renewal problem — each with its own shelf of books. This chapter makes a different claim, and it is the claim on which the whole of Movement IV rests: these are not three problems. They are one law, showing up at three scales. A soul cools, a household cools, a congregation cools — by the same dynamic, for the same reason, with the same remedy. The law can be stated in one sentence: Spiritual life runs downhill the moment it stops receiving — because created life was never self sustaining. Only the Source is. If that is true, it changes how a church prepares for revival, how it reads its own decline, and above all what it must build to keep whatever God sends. So let us take it slowly. Ruin Without a Vandal The New Testament has a verse that pictures the whole thing. "Therefore we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it" (Hebrews 2:1, ESV throughout). The word translated drift appears only here in the New Testament, and it paints a boat slipping its mooring — no storm, no leak, no hand on the rope. Just the current, and time. The drifting is passive, silent, and imperceptible while it is happening. And notice the direction built into the picture: no one drifts toward the harbor. The Old Testament paints the same law on land. The wise man walks past the field of the sluggard and finds thorns, nettles, and the stone wall broken down (Proverbs 24:30–34) — and here is the detail worth sitting with: nothing attacked that field. There is no destructive act anywhere in the account. Time plus inattention was sufficient. Ecclesiastes states it as a plain proverb of maintenance: "Through sloth the roof sinks in, and through indolence the house leaks" (Ecclesiastes 10:18). Ruin by commission needs a vandal. Ruin by omission needs only a calendar. That is the disturbing part of the drift, and also the diagnostic part: nobody has to do anything for it to happen. The believer whose prayer thinned did not decide anything. The family did not vote to lose the faith. The congregation never passed a motion to cool. Drift is what spiritual life does by default when the receiving stops — which is why it is always noticed late, and why Scripture's counsel against it comes with a dosage: "exhort one another every day, as long as it is called 'today,' that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" (Hebrews 3:13). Every day — because the current runs every day. Scripture even names the drift's favorite accelerant, and it is not persecution. It is prosperity. "Take care lest you forget the LORD your God... lest, when you have eaten and are full... then your heart be lifted up, and you forget the LORD your God" (Deuteronomy 8:11–14). Hard times drive a people to their knees; the full stomach quietly closes the valve. Notice, all through Deuteronomy, that remember is a

title: How Revivals Were Lost
text: How Revivals Were Lost Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. The previous chapter, The Drift Downward, named the law: spiritual life runs downhill the moment it stops receiving, at every scale, by default. This chapter opens the case file. Because the drift is not a theory — it has a history, and the history has names and dates. Wales, 1904. Northampton, 1735. Azusa Street, 1906. Cane Ridge, 1801. New York, 1957. Real fires fell in real churches, and in every case the record lets us watch what happened next — and in every case, sooner or later, something was lost. Read that record honestly and a pattern emerges that ought to change how a church prays for revival. The fires were not lost in exotic ways. They were not stolen by persecution, mostly, or crushed by hostile governments, or argued away by clever skeptics. They were lost in a handful of recurring, recognizable, almost boring ways — the same few failure modes, century after century, like a farmer losing harvest after harvest to the same unfixed fence. And here is this chapter's one sentence, the spine everything below hangs on: none of these failure modes is exotic — every one of them is a preparation question. Every mode in the record answers to something a church could have built, or asked, or settled before the rain fell. Which is why this book exists: not to teach you to summon the fire, which no one can, but to walk you through the preparation questions while there is still time to answer them. So take them one at a time. Six ways revivals were lost. For each: what happened, why it happens, and where in this book the answer lives. The Form Outlived the Power What happened. This is the oldest mode in the record and the most common. The revival comes; the revival gets a container — meetings, methods, buildings, schedules — as it must, for wine needs a skin. And then, over years, the life quietly departs while the container keeps running. The mission on Azusa Street, where the twentieth century's largest Christian movement was born in 1906, dwindled to a remnant and was gone by 1931 — while the movement it had birthed spread across the world. Welsh church membership peaked in 1905, in the very glow of the great revival, and declined continuously from that year forward — the structures full, then fuller, then hollowing. And behind these stand the two churches the risen Christ Himself diagnosed: Ephesus, every structure intact — works, toil, endurance, sound doctrine — and the first love abandoned; Sardis, with "the reputation of being alive," and dead (Revelation 3:1, ESV). Students of religious movements have watched this so consistently they gave it a name — the routinization of the extraordinary — and the record confirms it with almost mechanical regularity: the founding event fades; the forms remain; and the church's energy shifts, imperceptibly, from receiving the wine to polishing the skin. The mechanism, plainly. Structure does not need fresh inflow to persist, and life does. So given enough time, every untended structure outlives the life it was built to hold — and a congregation can maintain "the appearance of godliness" long after "denying its power" (2 Timothy 3:5, ESV), usually without ever noticing the exchange. Nobody votes to become Sardis. Sardis is what a revival becomes by default when the receiving stops and the meetings continue. The preparation that answers it. This one belongs to the chapter you have just read. The Drift Downward gives the whole discipline: structures examined on a schedule rather than at a crisis; fruit measured instead of polish; ministries retired honorably when the wine has moved on; and the receiving — hearing God, obeying God — kept ruthlessly at the center of everything. A church that installs those habits before the rain has answered, in advance, the failure mode that has taken more revivals than any other. The Harvest Grew the Thorns What happened. Jesus warned that among the soils

title: Handing the Fire
text: Handing the Fire Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Suppose the rain comes. Suppose God answers the prayers this book has been teaching you to pray — the soil softens, the fire falls, and your church is rearranged by a mercy it did not manufacture. Everything in this movement so far has been about keeping that fire through the ordinary drift of years. But one test remains, and it is the hardest, because it does not arrive for twenty years and cannot be crammed for the night before. The ultimate survival test of a revival is not the next budget year. It is the next generation. The record is unsentimental here. Revival's children inherit one of two things: living fire, or a photograph of one. They grow up either warming their hands at something real, or dusting a frame on the mantel — the building their grandparents built, the hymns their grandparents wept through, the vocabulary of an encounter they never had. And the difference between those inheritances is not luck. It follows patterns, and — this is the mercy in it — much of what matters can be built now, before any rain falls. This chapter is written for elders, yes. But just as much for parents and grandparents, because everything the research says points to one uncomfortable, liberating fact: the handoff does not happen in the sanctuary. It happens at your table. One Generation from Forgetting Start with the most sobering sentence in the book of Judges. Joshua's generation had seen it all — the sea opened, the Jordan cut off, the walls down. If any generation ever had a revival to hand down, it was this one. And then: "And all that generation also were gathered to their fathers. And there arose another generation after them who did not know the LORD or the work that he had done for Israel" (Judges 2:10, ESV). One generation. Not a slow slide across centuries — one funeral cycle between the greatest move of God in Israel's history and children who did not know the LORD . And before we blame distance, Scripture closes that exit too. Eli's sons were priests' sons, raised inside the sanctuary, handling the offerings of God with their own hands: "They did not know the LORD" (1 Samuel 2:12, ESV). Even young Samuel, sleeping steps from the ark, "did not yet know the LORD" (1 Samuel 3:7, ESV) — not until the night God called him by name and he answered for himself. There is the whole problem in three verses. Proximity to the things of God is not knowledge of God. You can raise a child inside the revival, and the child can still miss the LORD of it. Why? Because of something Scripture teaches plainly and modern research has spent fifty years confirming from the outside. What No Parent Can Hand Down Faith is not heritable. John's Gospel says it with three hammer blows: the children of God are born "not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God" (John 1:13, ESV). Not of blood — bloodline gets you nothing. Not of the will of the flesh, not of the will of man — no father's determination, however fierce, can will a child into the new birth. Only God. Ezekiel says it from another angle. Israel had a proverb — the fathers eat sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge — and God abolished it: "The son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father, nor the father suffer for the iniquity of the son" (Ezekiel 18:20, ESV). Guilt does not run in the blood — and, the same chapter's quieter corollary, neither does righteousness. Each soul stands before God on its own feet. And Jeremiah puts the end of hereditary religion inside the new covenant promise itself: "they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest" (Jeremiah 31:34, ESV). All of them — firsthand. Nobody knows God by being somebody's child. An old saying, often credited to Corrie ten Boom, puts it in five words: God has no grandchildren. The sociologists, with their decades long surveys, hit the same wall. Nobody can be born a volunteer

title: Movement IV — Keeping What Comes
text: Movement IV — Keeping What Comes Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Every revival in the historical record cooled. That single fact shapes this whole movement — because the cooling was not random. It followed patterns, the patterns are describable, and a church that studies them before the rain has a chance the churches of the record did not take. What this movement holds: The drift downward, named. The spine of this book: what the longer work behind it calls drift — the universal tendency of spiritual life, in a soul and in a church, to run downhill when it stops receiving. Why the fire fades by default; why maintaining yesterday's blessing takes today's obedience; why no church coasts upward. The failure modes of previous revivals. The recurring ways moves of God were lost: the form outliving the power (structures kept polishing themselves after the life had gone); success manufacturing the comfort and riches that choke the next generation (the thorns grow from the harvest); the movement housed in one leader who could not be told hard truth and could not be succeeded; the hungry converted and never formed. Each failure mode is paired with the preparation that answers it — most of which this book has already walked you through, which is the point. The wineskin. Jesus' warning that new wine bursts old wineskins, taken seriously as a law of institutions: every fresh work routinizes, and what keeps a wineskin supple is designed renewal — including a willingness to let structures (even this pilot's structures) be examined, refreshed, and if need be, retired. Handing the fire to the next generation. Faith is not heritable; the conditions of faith are. What the record and the research say about how revival's children either inherit living fire or a photograph of one — warmth as the carrier, honest questions welcomed rather than managed, and a household and church culture that provokes the next generation to ask for the real thing. The movement — and the book — closes where it began: with the two questions, now fully informed. Do you want a revival? And now that you know what the soil requires: will you begin?

title: How These Foundations Were Tested
text: How These Foundations Were Tested Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. A church weighing this proposal has a right to ask a blunt question: did anyone ever seriously challenge this before it reached us? Not "did the author believe it" — authors always believe it — but did anyone sit down with the foundations underneath this book and try, deliberately and at length, to break them? The answer is yes. This chapter tells you how it was done, what held, what did not, and what is still unfinished. It is offered as testimony of diligence, not as a boast — and it begins with an honest explanation of what this testing was, and what it was not. What this testing was — and what it was not Behind this book stand twelve foundation documents — the papers in Intentional Journey of the Heart that work out the convictions this proposal rests on: that ordinary believers can hear God and obey Him, that formation of the heart can be shepherded and even honestly observed, and that revival rises and drains away in recognizable patterns. Each of those twelve documents was examined through a structured internal review method. Here is how it worked, stated plainly. For each document, a panel of reviewer personas was assembled — each one written in the voice of a trusted figure whose lifework bears directly on the questions at stake: historical shepherds who actually kept watch over souls, theologians who thought hard about the church, measurement scientists who built and criticized instruments, and clinicians who spent careers with the wounded. Each persona was reconstructed from that figure's published work, and asked to challenge the document as that tradition would challenge it — with its hardest questions, not its polite ones. Then a separate adversarial verification pass took every finding the panel produced and tried to refute it against the document's actual text, page by page. Only findings that survived that hunt were counted. Now the part that must be said without any softening, because a church deserves total clarity here: these were exercises in perspective. They were designed to force the hardest questions each tradition would ask — nothing more, and nothing less. No living or historical person actually reviewed, approved, or endorsed any of these materials, and none should be understood to have done so. When this chapter says a review "in the voice of" a Puritan pastor or a trauma clinician found something, it means the discipline of asking, from within that person's published thought, the question that person would most likely press — and then checking whether the document could answer it. This is not peer review in the academic sense, and it does not pretend to be. (One of the foundation papers has separately been under review at an academic journal, through the ordinary blind process; that is a different track, and it is not what this chapter describes.) Why do it this way at all? Because a project written largely by one hand needs adversaries, and the best adversaries available were the great traditions themselves — the pastoral tradition that knows what oversight becomes when it curdles, the measurement tradition that knows what happens to any indicator once institutions start acting on it, the clinical tradition that has sat with the casualties of religious systems, the missionary tradition that asks what any of this is for . Writing their questions honestly, and then trying to refute the answers against the text, turned out to be a demanding exam. Whether it was demanding enough, you will be able to judge for yourself by the end of this chapter — because the results were not uniformly flattering. How the reviews worked The process ran one document at a time, twelve documents in all, over many weeks. For each document, the panel was matched to the material — a paper on measuring spiritual maturity drew measurement scientists, pastors who kept real records of real souls, and a clinician whose

title: What the Reviews Saw
text: What the Reviews Saw Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. Before this proposal was assembled, the twelve foundation documents behind it — the papers on hearing God, on the heart, on formation, on measuring and cultivating spiritual maturity — were each placed before a review panel with one instruction: challenge the basic assumptions. Try to break them. This chapter preserves what those reviews saw, because their insights were too valuable to lose to a summary, and because most of the hardest won pages in this book exist because a reviewer demanded them. First, though, you must know exactly what those panels were, because nothing in this chapter should be read without it. The reviews were a structured internal exercise in which each review was written in the voice of a trusted figure — historical shepherds like Richard Baxter, John Wesley, and Gregory the Great; theologians like Jonathan Edwards and Dietrich Bonhoeffer; scientists, measurement scholars, and clinicians — with each voice reconstructed, as faithfully as we could manage, from that figure's own published work. They are exercises in perspective: a disciplined way of asking, what would the author of "The Reformed Pastor" notice here that we cannot see? They are not endorsements. No living or historical person reviewed or approved these materials. When this chapter says "the review written in the voice of Richard Baxter," that phrase is the entire claim being made — no more. Every finding was then checked adversarially against the documents' actual text, and only what survived that check is reported here. What follows are the twenty insights that most shaped this book, sorted under the book's own themes. For each: the insight itself, why it matters to a church considering revival, and where it landed in these pages. The dry believer The single most persistent finding of all twelve reviews — surfacing, in one form or another, in eight of them — concerned the believer whose spiritual life has gone dry. No other theme came up from so many directions, and no other theme is closer to the heart of this book. 1. Dryness is not disconnection. Three reviews from traditions with almost nothing in common — written in the voices of Dietrich Bonhoeffer, of spiritual director David Benner, and of Dallas Willard — independently flagged the same teaching exercise in our materials: one that treated the felt sense of God's presence as a gauge of real connection with Him. The Benner voiced review put it unforgettably: "if connection with God is a feeling that can be recalled and installed, then the desert is a malfunction, and Mother Teresa was doing it wrong for fifty years. She was not." Why it matters: press a congregation toward God and some of them will go dry; if leaders equate dry with disconnected, they will wound exactly the people God is working deepest in. This warning built When the Ground Goes Dry and stands behind the "dryness that aches" passage of Hungry Hearts. 2. Dryness is at least four things — and two of the remedies are opposites. The reviews written in the voice of Ignatius of Loyola pressed this on nearly every document that touched the interior life: dryness may be simple neglect, or exhausting over obligation, or the enemy's desolation (to be resisted), or God's own stripping (to be received) — and a leader who holds only one category will sooner or later invert the counsel, fighting what God is doing or yielding to what the enemy is doing. One such review warned that without this distinction, any diagnostic "will be most wrong about the souls God is most at work in." The four cases of When the Ground Goes Dry are this finding, answered. 3. The night falls where God sends it — including on beginners. The review written in the voice of Brother Lawrence, whose own long darkness came at the start of his walk with God, protested any map that files the dark seasons under advanced sainthood: a young soul in that trouble "will

title: Reference
text: Reference Draft status: working draft (DEV) — offered for prayer and honest discussion. This section holds the deeper material behind the four movements, for readers — especially leaders — who want to see the foundations. What this section holds: The research behind each movement — standalone treatments of the revival record, the seven churches diagnostics, the desolation differential, the drift and decay dynamics, and formation across generations, each readable on its own and each pointing into Intentional Journey of the Heart for the full scholarly base. How these foundations were tested. Before this proposal was assembled, the twelve foundation documents behind it were each placed before an independent review panel — voices from measurement science, pastoral practice, clinical trauma care, missiology, and church history, asked to challenge the basic assumptions. A summary chapter will report what held (the core conviction that ordinary believers can hear and obey God survived all twelve reviews) and what the reviewers demanded (most of the guardrails in Movement II exist because reviewers insisted on them). The reviewers' insights, preserved. The individual review findings were too valuable to lose to summary — the ones that shaped this book will be kept here as reference reading, credited to the perspective that produced them. Pointers and further reading — where each thread continues in Intentional Journey of the Heart and in the Fellowship of the Heart pilot materials.

title: Start here
text: Start Here Draft status: working draft (DEV) — structure and content still forming. Who this book is for It is written for a church — first for its leadership (pastors, elders, and those who carry responsibility for the congregation's direction), and then for its small group leaders and any member who wants to read along. You do not need any background in the larger body of work this proposal draws from. Where deeper foundations exist, the book points to them; it never requires them. What this book is — and is not It is a proposal for a pilot. Not a program to install, but a path a willing church could walk: an honest season of asking whether we want revival, discovering the state of our soil, preparing it, and learning — in advance — how churches before us received a move of God and then lost it. It is not a technique for producing revival. No such technique exists. The whole record of the church says the fire falls where God wills. The same record says, just as clearly, that the falling fire found some houses ready and some not — and that readiness had a describable shape. How the book is organized Four movements, in order, each building on the one before: 1. Do You Want a Revival? — the question itself, with the cost on the table. 2. What Is Your Soil? — diagnosis before cultivation, with strong guardrails. 3. Preparing the Soil — the practices, beginning with the leadership's own hearts. 4. Keeping What Comes — sustainment, and the failure modes of the past. Then a Reference section holding the deeper material: the research behind each movement, a summary of how these foundations were tested, and pointers into Intentional Journey of the Heart — the reference base this book stands on — and Fellowship of the Heart , the small group pilot this book will lean on for its leadership formation mechanics. The order matters One conviction shapes the sequence of this book more than any other: preparation starts at the top. A congregation's soil is tilled from the leadership downward — a leadership that has itself become a fellowship of the heart, before it asks anyone else to become one. Movement III is built around that order, and it is the hardest and most important thing in this proposal. Reading time The four movements are deliberately short enough for a leadership team to read together across a handful of meetings, one movement at a time, with discussion. The Reference section is there for whoever wants to go further.
